channel.title: Nature
channel.description: Nature is the foremost international weekly scientific journal in the world and is the flagship journal for Nature Portfolio. It publishes the finest peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nature publishes landmark papers, award winning news, leading comment and expert opinion on important, topical scientific news and events that enable readers to share the latest discoveries in science and evolve the discussion amongst the global scientific community.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Retraction Note: Multisensory learning binds neurons into a cross-modal memory engram
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10355-4

Retraction Note: Multisensory learning binds neurons into a cross-modal memory engram
item.dc:title: Retraction Note: Multisensory learning binds neurons into a cross-modal memory engram
item.dc:creator: Zeynep Okray
Pedro F. Jacob
Ciara Stern
Kieran Desmond
Nils Otto
Clifford B. Talbot
Paola Vargas-Gutierrez
Scott Waddell

item.title: Why insects aren't huge: a new challenge to a decades-old idea
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00976-0

Evidence suggests oxygen levels may not have been limiting factor — plus, CRISPR gives cancer-fighting immune cells an upgrade.
item.dc:title: Why insects aren't huge: a new challenge to a decades-old idea
item.dc:creator: Nick Petrić Howe
Maren Hunsberger

item.title: Can China keep up its extraordinary research growth?
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00423-0

Evidence of a pivot towards other sources of funding and collaboration suggest it is looking for ways to maintain its rapid pace of change.
item.dc:title: Can China keep up its extraordinary research growth?
item.dc:creator: Simon Baker

item.title: Genomic history of early dogs in Europe
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10112-7

Genome-wide analysis shows European dogs existed by 14,200 years ago, were already genetically distinct, received less Neolithic Southwest Asian admixture than humans did and contributed substantially to later European dogs.
item.dc:title: Genomic history of early dogs in Europe
item.dc:creator: Anders Bergström
Anja Furtwängler
Sarah Johnston
Erika Rosengren
Abagail Breidenstein

Pontus Skoglund

item.title: Dominant clones leverage developmental epigenomic states to drive ependymoma
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10270-8

Single-nucleus chromatin and RNA sequencing identifies epigenetic chromatin domains that confer vulnerability to paediatric brain tumours such as ependymomas, providing insight into the development of such tumours despite ‘quiet’ genomes.
item.dc:title: Dominant clones leverage developmental epigenomic states to drive ependymoma
item.dc:creator: Alisha S. Kardian
Hua Sun
Siri Ippagunta
Nicholas Laboe
Srinidhi Varadharajan
Kwanha Yu
Hsiao-Chi Chen
Erik Emanus

Yong-Dong Wang

Gabriele Kembuan
Tristen Wright
Emma Caesar
Maxwell Park
Amelia Hancock
David Gee
Joel Murdoch
Yi Xiao

Thomas E. Merchant
Jun Qi
Adam D. Durbin
Lindsay A. Schwarz
Li Wang
Andrew M. Donson
Nicholas K. Foreman
Sameer Agnihotri
Alfonso Lavado
Suzanne J. Baker
David W. Ellison
Hyun Kyoung Lee
Shondra M. Pruett-Miller
Kelsey C. Bertrand
Benjamin Deneen
Stephen C. Mack

item.title: Ectopic NMDAR expression in cancer unmasks germline-encoded autoimmunity
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10278-0

NMDAR expression is sufficient to induce B cell recruitment and affinity maturation, resulting in receptor-modulating antibodies that connect anti-cancer immunity and autoimmunity.
item.dc:title: Ectopic NMDAR expression in cancer unmasks germline-encoded autoimmunity
item.dc:creator: Sam O. Kleeman
Kevin Michalski
Xiang Zhao
Ruben Steigerwald
Miriam Ferrer
Llewelyn Levett
Ethan Ertel
Austin Schultz
Noriko Simorowski
Pamela Moody
Tse-Luen Wee
Cristina Valente
Sharon Fox
Mateusz Makuch
Selina Thomsen
Ruby Harrison
Claire Regan
Jonathan Preall
Qing Gao
Dennis Thomas
Jill Habel
Rachel Rubino
Sarosh Irani
Hiro Furukawa
Tobias Janowitz

item.title: Exposed phosphatidylserine is an inhibitory molecule in T cell exhaustion
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10266-4

Insights into the mechanism by which phosphatidylserine functions as a non-classical inhibitory molecule during T cell exhaustion, and how phosphatidylserine-targeting antibodies enhance T cell responses are explored.
item.dc:title: Exposed phosphatidylserine is an inhibitory molecule in T cell exhaustion
item.dc:creator: Christopher B. Medina
Ewelina Sobierajska
Minghao Gong
Daniel T. McManus
Maheshwor Thapa
Judong Lee
Se Jin Im
Jason E. Toombs
Joshua M. Mitchell
Yating Wang
Jennifer W. Carlisle
Gordon J. Freeman
Viraj A. Master
Suresh S. Ramalingam
Haydn T. Kissick
Shuzhao Li
Rolf A. Brekken
Rafi Ahmed

item.title: Functional hierarchy of the human neocortex across the lifespan
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10219-x

fMRI data from individuals of a wide range of ages (from a few days to 100 years old) are used to map the key organizational axes of functional connectivity in the human cortex throughout the lifespan.
item.dc:title: Functional hierarchy of the human neocortex across the lifespan
item.dc:creator: Hoyt Patrick Taylor IV
Khoi Minh Huynh
Kim-Han Thung
Guoye Lin
Wenjiao Lyu
Weili Lin
Sahar Ahmad
Pew-Thian Yap

item.title: Towards intelligent and miniaturized drug delivery devices
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10221-3

Intelligent and miniaturized drug delivery devices leveraging advances in biotechnology, artificial intelligence, electronics and materials science enable treatments with increased precision and responsiveness, with applications in cancer, diabetes, cardiovascular disease and other diseases.
item.dc:title: Towards intelligent and miniaturized drug delivery devices
item.dc:creator: Xinwei Wei
John B. Buse
Hongming Chen
Tejal A. Desai
Molly M. Stevens
Giovanni Traverso
Robert Langer
Zhen Gu

item.title: Aversive learning hijacks a brain sugar sensor to consolidate memory
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10306-z

A study in Drosophilareveals a mechanism of non-homeostatic hunger and its critical relevance for memory consolidation.
item.dc:title: Aversive learning hijacks a brain sugar sensor to consolidate memory
item.dc:creator: Raquel Francés
Typhaine Comyn
Coraline Desnous
Francesca Bettoni
Alice Pavlowsky
Thomas Preat
Pierre-Yves Plaçais

item.title: Inactivating SnRK1β1A promotes broad-spectrum disease resistance in rice
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10273-5

SnRK1β1A in rice promotes susceptibility to multiple fungal diseases, and disrupting this infection-inducible gene confers broad-spectrum resistance without compromising growth or yield under normal field conditions.
item.dc:title: Inactivating SnRK1β1A promotes broad-spectrum disease resistance in rice
item.dc:creator: Guixin Yuan
Xunli Lu
Xingbin Wang
Mengfei Li
Shiwei Wang
Zhaoxiang Huang
Zhigang Li
Fengrui Zhang
Xin Zhang
Jun Yang
Hailong Guo
Vijai Bhadauria
Wang-Sheng Zhu
Wensheng Zhao
Meng Yuan
Jian-Min Zhou
You-Liang Peng

item.title: Topological soliton frequency comb in nanophotonic lithium niobate
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10292-2

An integrated lithium niobate–semiconductor platform generates topological soliton frequency combs on-chip, enabling turn-key, dispersion-agnostic comb sources without high-Q resonators or complex stabilization.
item.dc:title: Topological soliton frequency comb in nanophotonic lithium niobate
item.dc:creator: Nicolas Englebert
Robert M. Gray
Luis Ledezma
Ryoto Sekine
Thomas Zacharias
Rithvik Ramesh
Benjamin K. Gutierrez
Pedro Parra-Rivas
Alireza Marandi

item.title: Structural energetics of cold sensitivity
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10276-2

Data from cryogenic electron microscopy combined with hydrogen–deuterium exchange mass spectrometry inform a mechanism for cold-evoked activation of the TRPM8 channel, providing a structural and energetic framework to explain cold sensitivity.
item.dc:title: Structural energetics of cold sensitivity
item.dc:creator: Kevin Y. Choi
Xiaoxuan Lin
Yifan Cheng
David Julius

item.title: A fast starburst wind consumes most of the energy from supernovae
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10231-1

Starburst galaxies are seen to host galaxy-scale winds, which are super-fast and could be powered entirely by the thermal pressure of gas heated by supernovae.
item.dc:title: A fast starburst wind consumes most of the energy from supernovae
item.dc:creator: Marc Audard
Hisamitsu Awaki
Ralf Ballhausen

item.title: Androgen activity in the male embryonic hindbrain drives lethal PFA ependymoma
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10264-6

Androgen activity in the male embryonic hindbrain prolongs hindbrain differentiation in male individuals and drives sex differences in the incidence and prognosis of posterior fossa type A (PFA) ependymoma, an aggressive childhood brain tumour.
item.dc:title: Androgen activity in the male embryonic hindbrain drives lethal PFA ependymoma
item.dc:creator: Jiao Zhang
Winnie Ong
Alexandra Rasnitsyn
Ricardo Daniel Gonzalez
Rodrigo Lopez Gutierrez

Michael D. Taylor

item.title: The DNA virome varies with human genes and environments
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10288-y

Analyses of biobank data show that human variation such as age, sex and genetics, particularly at the major histocompatibility complex locus, is associated with viral abundance and supports a causal link between abundance of Epstein–Barr virus and Hodgkin’s lymphoma.
item.dc:title: The DNA virome varies with human genes and environments
item.dc:creator: Nolan Kamitaki
David Tang
Steven A. McCarroll
Po-Ru Loh

item.title: Rapid concerted switching of the neural code in the inferotemporal cortex
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10267-3

Face cells in the macaque inferotemporal cortex are initially able to detect faces and then rapidly switch to a face-specific neural code to discriminate between different face identities.
item.dc:title: Rapid concerted switching of the neural code in the inferotemporal cortex
item.dc:creator: Yuelin Shi
Dasheng Bi
Janis K. Hesse
Frank F. Lanfranchi
Shi Chen
Doris Y. Tsao

item.title: Structural basis of supercoiling-induced CRISPR–Cas9 off-target activity
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10255-7

Cas9 structures explain topology sensing and off-target activation.
item.dc:title: Structural basis of supercoiling-induced CRISPR–Cas9 off-target activity
item.dc:creator: Quentin M. Smith
Sylvia Whittle
Ricardo J. Aramayo
Daniel E. Rollins
Adam S. B. Jalal
Deborah I. Egharevba
Kyle L. Morris
Alice L. B. Pyne
David S. Rueda

item.title: Dogs were widely distributed across western Eurasia during the Palaeolithic
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10170-x

Analysis of nuclear and mitochondrial genomes from archaeological canid remains found across Europe and Anatolia shows that a genetically homogeneous dog population was already widely distributed across the region by 15,000 years ago.
item.dc:title: Dogs were widely distributed across western Eurasia during the Palaeolithic
item.dc:creator: William A. Marsh
Lachie Scarsbrook
Eren Yüncü
Lizzie Hodgson
Audrey T. Lin
Maria De Iorio
Olaf Thalmann

Sophy Charlton
Greger Larson
Ian Barnes
Laurent A. F. Frantz

item.title: CO<sub>2</sub> subsurface mineral storage by its co-injection with recirculating water
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10130-5

An industrial-scale pilot project using co-injection of recirculated water for subsurface CO2 mineralization shows promise as a pathway for carbon sequestration in regions with limited access to water resources.
item.dc:title: CO<sub>2</sub> subsurface mineral storage by its co-injection with recirculating water
item.dc:creator: Eric H. Oelkers
Serguey Arkadakskiy
Zeyad Ahmed
Noushad Kunnummal
Jakub Fedorik
Massimo Marchesi
Mouadh Addassi
Abdirizak Omar
Niccolo Menegoni
Sigurdur R. Gislason
Grimur Bjornsson
Davide Berno
Thomas Finkbeiner
Abdulkader Afifi
Hussein Hoteit

item.title: Electrochemical corrosion accompanies dendrite growth in solid electrolytes
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10279-z

Operando birefringence microscopy measurements of the stresses around growing dendrites in solid electrolytes show that stresses decrease as current densities increase, revealing a linkage between electrochemical and mechanical stability that informs the design of solid-state batteries.
item.dc:title: Electrochemical corrosion accompanies dendrite growth in solid electrolytes
item.dc:creator: Cole D. Fincher
Colin Gilgenbach
Christian Roach
Rachel Osmundsen
Aubrey Penn
Michael D. Thouless
W. Craig Carter
Brian W. Sheldon
James M. LeBeau
Yet-Ming Chiang

item.title: Parasites trigger epithelial cell crosstalk to drive gut–brain signalling
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10281-5

Paracrine signalling between tuft cells and enterochromaffin cells is a key mode of immune–sensory and gut–brain communication, and accounts for the pattern of gastrointestinal symptoms that occurs during parasite infections.
item.dc:title: Parasites trigger epithelial cell crosstalk to drive gut–brain signalling
item.dc:creator: Kouki K. Touhara
Jinhao Xu
Joel Castro
Hong-Erh Liang
Guochuan Li
Mariana Brizuela
Andrea M. Harrington
Sonia Garcia-Caraballo
Tracey O’Donnell
Daniel Neumann
Nathan D. Rossen
Fei Deng
Gudrun Schober
Yulong Li
Richard M. Locksley
Stuart M. Brierley
David Julius

item.title: Epigenetic memory of colitis promotes tumour growth
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10258-4

Colonic stem cells retain a memory of inflammation following disease resolution and there is a mechanistic link between chronic inflammation and malignancy, suggesting potential strategies to mitigate cancer risk in patients with chronic inflammatory conditions.
item.dc:title: Epigenetic memory of colitis promotes tumour growth
item.dc:creator: Surya Nagaraja
Lety Ojeda-Miron
Ruochi Zhang
Ena Oreskovic
Conrad Hock
Yan Hu
Daniel Zeve
Karina Sharma
Roni R. Hyman
Qiming Zhang
Andrew Castillo
David T. Breault
Ömer H. Yilmaz
Jason D. Buenrostro

item.title: Towards end-to-end automation of AI research
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10265-5

An artificial intelligence system can produce research papers with minimal human involvement, even passing the first round of peer review for the workshop of a main machine learning conference.
item.dc:title: Towards end-to-end automation of AI research
item.dc:creator: Chris Lu
Cong Lu
Robert Tjarko Lange
Yutaro Yamada
Shengran Hu
Jakob Foerster
David Ha
Jeff Clune

item.title: Disequilibrium response to tapping crustal magma reveals storage conditions
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10317-w

Magma drilling data from Krafla volcano, Iceland, are used to reconstruct in situ lithostatic magmatic conditions using disequilibrium simulations that provide a method for improving the understanding of magma storage conditions and evolution.
item.dc:title: Disequilibrium response to tapping crustal magma reveals storage conditions
item.dc:creator: Janine Birnbaum
Fabian B. Wadsworth
Jackie E. Kendrick
Ben Kennedy
Paul A. Wallace
Marize Muniz da Silva
Kai-Uwe Hess
Yan Lavallée

item.title: Precipitation observing network gaps limit climate change impact assessment
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10300-5

At present, only 13.4% of the global land surface meets the World Meteorological Organization requirements for annual precipitation monitoring.
item.dc:title: Precipitation observing network gaps limit climate change impact assessment
item.dc:creator: Jiajia Su
Chiyuan Miao
Francis Zwiers
Hylke Beck
Phil Jones
Qiaohong Sun
Louise J. Slater
Wouter R. Berghuijs
Yoshihide Wada
Daniel Rosenfeld
Jiaojiao Gou
Yi Wu
Paolo Tarolli
Pasquale Borrelli
Panos Panagos
Lisa V. Alexander
Qi Zhang
Jinlong Hu
Seung-Ki Min
Luis Samaniego
Qingyun Duan
Georgia Destouni
Jose A. Marengo
Reza Modarres
Soroosh Sorooshian

item.title: Quantifying climate loss and damage consistent with a social cost of carbon
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10272-6

A new framework links specific emissions to monetized, location-specific climate damages, showing that future harms from past CO2 emissions far exceed historical damages and that delayed carbon removal cannot fully offset these losses.
item.dc:title: Quantifying climate loss and damage consistent with a social cost of carbon
item.dc:creator: Marshall Burke
Mustafa Zahid
Noah S. Diffenbaugh
Solomon Hsiang

item.title: Oxygen supply through the tracheolar–muscle system does not constrain insect gigantism
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10291-3

New evidence suggests that diffusive oxygen transport through the tracheolar–muscle system is not the limiting factor on insect body size.
item.dc:title: Oxygen supply through the tracheolar–muscle system does not constrain insect gigantism
item.dc:creator: Edward P. Snelling
Antonia V. Lensink
Susana Clusella-Trullas
Chris Weldon
Philipp Lehmann
John S. Terblanche
Nicholas L. Payne
Jon F. Harrison
Anthony J. R. Hickey
Ashleigh Donaldson
Christian M. Deschodt
Roger S. Seymour

item.title: Decadal-scale droughts disrupted the African Humid Period in the Sahara
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10336-7

Sedimentary time-series data of Lake Yoa in Chad covering the past 10.25 thousand years (kyr) show that the mid-Holocene African Humid Period experienced several decadal-scale droughts, caused by sudden inputs of freshwater into the North Atlantic.
item.dc:title: Decadal-scale droughts disrupted the African Humid Period in the Sahara
item.dc:creator: Florence Sylvestre
Martin Melles
Volker Wennrich
Michèle Dinies
Françoise Chalié
Didier Swingedouw
Anne Dallmeyer
Xiaoxu Shi
Martin Claussen
Andrea Jaeschke
Christine Cocquyt
Jens Karls
Jan Kuper
Baba Mallaye
Jean-Charles Mazur
Christine Paillès
Remadji Rirongarti
Janet Rethemeyer
Benedikt Ritter-Prinz
Enno Schefuß
Finn Viehberg
Bernd Wagner
Martin Werner
Abdallah N. Yacoub
Stefan Kröpelin

item.title: Moderate global warming does not rule out extreme global climate outcomes
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10237-9

Extreme global climate outcomes may occur even under moderate 2 °C warming and may turn out to be more extreme than model-averaged projections at 3 °C or 4 °C warming.
item.dc:title: Moderate global warming does not rule out extreme global climate outcomes
item.dc:creator: Emanuele Bevacqua
Erich Fischer
Jana Sillmann
Jakob Zscheischler

item.title: Superluminal correlations in ensembles of optical phase singularities
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10209-z

Ultrafast electron imaging shows full phase-space dynamics of optical singularities, which can reach superluminal velocities before annihilation and break the particle-like analogy of topological defects.
item.dc:title: Superluminal correlations in ensembles of optical phase singularities
item.dc:creator: T. Bucher
A. Gorlach
A. Niedermayr
Q. Yan
H. Nahari
K. Wang
R. Ruimy
Y. Adiv
M. Yannai
T. L. Abudi
E. Janzen
C. Spaegele
C. Roques-Carmes
J. H. Edgar
F. H. L. Koppens
G. M. Vanacore
H. H. Sheinfux
S. Tsesses
I. Kaminer

item.title: Redirecting current solves a shadowy problem faced by perovskite solar cells
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00921-1

Solar modules made from perovskite materials have a stability issue that arises when they are placed in partial shade. This long-standing problem has now been addressed by integrating an electronic component called a memristor into the solar cell.
item.dc:title: Redirecting current solves a shadowy problem faced by perovskite solar cells

item.title: China is an innovation powerhouse — but it should do more fundamental research
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00935-9

As the country looks to commercial companies to drive innovation, it has a golden opportunity to support open science and increase its output in key areas.
item.dc:title: China is an innovation powerhouse — but it should do more fundamental research

item.title: Major conference catches illicit AI use — and rejects hundreds of papers
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00893-2

The papers’ watermarks allowed organizers to detect use of large language models in peer review.
item.dc:title: Major conference catches illicit AI use — and rejects hundreds of papers
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Gibney

item.title: Remembrance of inflammations past
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00639-0

Chronic inflammation increases the risk of colon cancer. This inflammation drives epigenetic changes in the nucleus of stem cells that promote tumour formation.
item.dc:title: Remembrance of inflammations past
item.dc:creator: Chengxiang Qiu
Jay Shendure

item.title: ‘Continuity over novelty’: why environmental science needs to rethink its focus
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00933-x

With government funding in decline, researchers should prioritize data collation and training the next generation of scientists.
item.dc:title: ‘Continuity over novelty’: why environmental science needs to rethink its focus
item.dc:creator: Michael Paul Nelson

item.title: Anticancer antibodies can evolve to cause autoimmune brain disease
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00919-9

A model of breast cancer that expresses NMDA receptors — proteins that modify signalling between neurons — was used to trace pre-existing B cells as they matured to produce autoimmune antibodies that have diverse effects on receptor function. Transferring one antibody into mouse brains recapitulated key features of autoimmune brain inflammation.
item.dc:title: Anticancer antibodies can evolve to cause autoimmune brain disease

item.title: Why China’s philanthropists are digging deep for research
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00424-z

An increase in donations, prizes and public–private initiatives suggests many firms want to show commitment to the government’s goals.
item.dc:title: Why China’s philanthropists are digging deep for research
item.dc:creator: Ben Deighton

item.title: Who let the wolves in? Genetic record for domestic dogs pushed back by 5,000 years
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00900-6

The oldest ancient dog genomes on record all come from a population that lived alongside Ice Age hunter-gatherers across Europe and the Middle East.
item.dc:title: Who let the wolves in? Genetic record for domestic dogs pushed back by 5,000 years
item.dc:creator: Ewen Callaway

item.title: A guide to the Nature Index
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00427-w

A description of the terminology and methodology used in this supplement, and a guide to the functionality that is available free online at natureindex.com.
item.dc:title: A guide to the Nature Index

item.title: ‘Grade inflation’ hits PhD students. What’s behind the increase?
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00952-8

Study of one US university shows grades rising for master’s and PhD students over the past two decades.
item.dc:title: ‘Grade inflation’ hits PhD students. What’s behind the increase?
item.dc:creator: Mariana Lenharo

item.title: A cancer-promoting fusion protein acts during embryonic brain development
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00924-y

ZFTA–RELA is the cancer-promoting protein product of a gene fusion. Analyses of accessible sites in the DNA–protein complex chromatin in developing mice show that ZFTA–RELA binds to chromatin modules that are accessible during embryonic brain development, placing some cell lineages at risk of transformation into cancer.
item.dc:title: A cancer-promoting fusion protein acts during embryonic brain development

item.title: Charting the human brain’s lifelong functional organization
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00637-2

Neuroscientists have created a continuous atlas showing how patterns of functional connectivity between brain regions change from birth to old age.
item.dc:title: Charting the human brain’s lifelong functional organization
item.dc:creator: Richard A. I. Bethlehem
Daniel S. Margulies

item.title: Long-distance quantum link generates entanglement faster than it is lost
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00804-5

Entanglement between ions connected by 10 kilometres of optical fibre is a step towards large-scale quantum communications networks.
item.dc:title: Long-distance quantum link generates entanglement faster than it is lost
item.dc:creator: Ronald Hanson
Tracy Northup

item.title: Dogs have deep genetic roots in ice-age Europe
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00378-2

Two studies report the oldest dog genomes ever to be sequenced, representing leaps in scientists’ understanding of the animal’s origins.
item.dc:title: Dogs have deep genetic roots in ice-age Europe
item.dc:creator: Lauren M. Hennelly
Mikkel-Holger S. Sinding

item.title: How to build an AI Scientist: first peer-reviewed paper spills the secrets
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00899-w

AI Scientist, an autonomous research tool, first released in 2024, has now undergone peer review, highlighting its strengths and limitations.
item.dc:title: How to build an AI Scientist: first peer-reviewed paper spills the secrets
item.dc:creator: Davide Castelvecchi

item.title: AI scientists are changing research — institutions, funders and publishers must respond
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00934-w

The ability to automate the discovery process in some areas of scientific inquiry raises unanswered questions about how research should be conducted.
item.dc:title: AI scientists are changing research — institutions, funders and publishers must respond

item.title: The pros and cons of China’s health role in Africa
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00425-y

Funds and expertise are a welcome boost, but questions remain about balanced cooperation.
item.dc:title: The pros and cons of China’s health role in Africa
item.dc:creator: Sarah Wild

item.title: The surprising science behind red-light therapy — and how it really works
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00878-1

People are buying helmets, face masks, vests and beds that emit long-wavelength light. Beneath the hype, there is some interesting biology.
item.dc:title: The surprising science behind red-light therapy — and how it really works
item.dc:creator: Lynne Peeples

item.title: Pilot project paves way to storing CO<sub>2</sub> underground as minerals in arid countries
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00635-4

Carbon dioxide has been turned into minerals in Earth’s subsurface using recirculating water flow — a viable way to sequester this greenhouse gas in water-scarce areas.
item.dc:title: Pilot project paves way to storing CO<sub>2</sub> underground as minerals in arid countries
item.dc:creator: Juliane Weber

item.title: Testosterone promotes growth of a type of brain tumour in young boys
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00638-1

A type of childhood brain tumour called ependymoma is more common in boys than in girls. The reason for this difference turns out to be sex hormones such as testosterone.
item.dc:title: Testosterone promotes growth of a type of brain tumour in young boys
item.dc:creator: Najla Kfoury-Beaumont

item.title: First atlas of brain organization shows development over a lifetime
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00975-1

Scans of more than 3,500 people allow scientists to draw up a guide to the brain areas that work together from birth to 100 years old.
item.dc:title: First atlas of brain organization shows development over a lifetime
item.dc:creator: Gemma Conroy

item.title: Sock. Something. Um.
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00614-9

A fair exchange?
item.dc:title: Sock. Something. Um.
item.dc:creator: Daniel Roop

item.title: Geopolitical tensions are leading China to rethink research collaboration
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00426-x

A shift to regional partnerships and use of tightly controlled platforms suggests openness is being designed and managed.
item.dc:title: Geopolitical tensions are leading China to rethink research collaboration
item.dc:creator: Futao Huang

item.title: Extreme climate outcomes could still occur with just 2 °C of global warming
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00640-7

Moderate global warming creates a risk of climate impacts that are more severe than the most-likely impacts of high global warming.
item.dc:title: Extreme climate outcomes could still occur with just 2 °C of global warming
item.dc:creator: Rachel Warren

item.title: Author Correction: 7-Dehydrocholesterol is an endogenous suppressor of ferroptosis
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10403-z

Author Correction: 7-Dehydrocholesterol is an endogenous suppressor of ferroptosis
item.dc:title: Author Correction: 7-Dehydrocholesterol is an endogenous suppressor of ferroptosis

item.title: Marine life is a silent casualty of armed conflicts
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00942-w

Marine life is a silent casualty of armed conflicts
item.dc:title: Marine life is a silent casualty of armed conflicts
item.dc:creator: Seithikurippu R. Pandi-Perumal
Konda Mani Saravanan

item.title: Scientists should join collaborative online editing communities for biodiversity
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00940-y

Scientists should join collaborative online editing communities for biodiversity
item.dc:title: Scientists should join collaborative online editing communities for biodiversity
item.dc:creator: Heidi M. Meudt
Siobhan Leachman
Tiago Lubiana
Sabine von Mering

item.title: Why it’s hard to guess the high note
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00644-3

An explanation of the difficulty in judging musical tones, and colourful rings spotted around the Sun, in this week’s pick from the Nature archive.
item.dc:title: Why it’s hard to guess the high note

item.title: Salt lakes are shrinking and expanding, causing havoc in conservation
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00943-9

Salt lakes are shrinking and expanding, causing havoc in conservation
item.dc:title: Salt lakes are shrinking and expanding, causing havoc in conservation
item.dc:creator: Egor Zadereev

item.title: UK scraps airborne lab that tracks climate, pollution and weather systems
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00941-x

UK scraps airborne lab that tracks climate, pollution and weather systems
item.dc:title: UK scraps airborne lab that tracks climate, pollution and weather systems
item.dc:creator: Euan G. Nisbet
Maria R. Russo

item.title: ‘Zombie cells’ return from the dead — after a genome transplant
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00938-6

Technique that inserts the genome of one bacterial species into the ‘dead’ cells of another could open doors for synthetic biologists.
item.dc:title: ‘Zombie cells’ return from the dead — after a genome transplant
item.dc:creator: Ewen Callaway

item.title: Can a mouse be cloned indefinitely? Decades-long experiment has answers
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00945-7

Repeated cloning from a single mouse failed after 58 generations. Researchers say harmful DNA changes are to blame.
item.dc:title: Can a mouse be cloned indefinitely? Decades-long experiment has answers
item.dc:creator: Heidi Ledford

item.title: Antimatter has been transported for the first time ever — in the back of CERN’s truck
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00950-w

Physicists have succeeded for the first time in transporting the most expensive and most volatile substance on Earth: antimatter.
item.dc:title: Antimatter has been transported for the first time ever — in the back of CERN’s truck
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Gibney

item.title: These medical X-rays are all deepfakes — and they fool even radiologists
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00892-3

Radiologists and large language models alike have a hard time picking out real medical images from fake ones.
item.dc:title: These medical X-rays are all deepfakes — and they fool even radiologists
item.dc:creator: Jenna Ahart

item.title: How the war in Iran is reshaping the energy landscape
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00932-y

As prices surge and governments scramble to respond, the crisis underscores an urgent need to rethink energy security.
item.dc:title: How the war in Iran is reshaping the energy landscape
item.dc:creator: Aisha Al-Sarihi

item.title: Daily briefing: Tiny bones from Neanderthal fetus point to downfall of the species
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00963-5

A genetic bottleneck appears to have been behind the downfall of the Neanderthals. Plus, AI-generated medical X-rays are getting too realistic to spot and ways to grapple with a tsunami of experimental data.
item.dc:title: Daily briefing: Tiny bones from Neanderthal fetus point to downfall of the species
item.dc:creator: Flora Graham

item.title: Why AI hasn’t caused a job apocalypse — so far
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00883-4

Current evidence points to modest effects of artificial-intelligence tools on jobs, highlighting how bad data, not sweeping automation, is driving much of today’s alarm.
item.dc:title: Why AI hasn’t caused a job apocalypse — so far
item.dc:creator: Martha Gimbel

item.title: How DNA in dirt is shaking up the study of human origins
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00879-0

Researchers are pulling clues from genetic material in ice age soils and rewriting chapters of human history.
item.dc:title: How DNA in dirt is shaking up the study of human origins
item.dc:creator: Dyani Lewis

item.title: How the idea of human superiority over nature was invented
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 23 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00881-6

Scientific discoveries are increasingly eroding the human–animal divide and challenging long-standing assumptions about human exceptionalism.
item.dc:title: How the idea of human superiority over nature was invented
item.dc:creator: Brock Bastian

item.title: Daily briefing: We’ve just had the 11 hottest years on record
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 23 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00957-3

A new report shows that Earth’s climate is more out of balance than at any other time in recorded history. Plus, a lab-grown oesophagus and evidence that sperm whales headbutt.
item.dc:title: Daily briefing: We’ve just had the 11 hottest years on record
item.dc:creator: Flora Graham

item.title: How to measure a good life – tips for moving beyond GDP
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 23 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00884-3

Including human and environmental capital paints a different picture of national economies than does GDP alone.
item.dc:title: How to measure a good life – tips for moving beyond GDP
item.dc:creator: Richard Heys
Himanshi Bhardwaj
Cliodhna Taylor

item.title: How I squeeze fresh science from public data
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 23 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00434-x

For cash-strapped and early-career researchers, archived data sets can fuel new science.
item.dc:title: How I squeeze fresh science from public data
item.dc:creator: Rhys H. Parry

item.title: The intelligence illusion: why AI isn’t as smart as it is made out to be
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 23 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00882-5

Artificial-intelligence models will supposedly take over the world, but AI innovator Luc Julia tells Nature that they’re little more than glorified pocket calculators.
item.dc:title: The intelligence illusion: why AI isn’t as smart as it is made out to be
item.dc:creator: David Adam

item.title: The world just lived through the 11 hottest years on record — what now?
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 23 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00946-6

Earth is increasingly ‘out of balance’, as more heat is trapped in the atmosphere, driving global warming.
item.dc:title: The world just lived through the 11 hottest years on record — what now?
item.dc:creator: Rachel Fieldhouse
Mohana Basu
